security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate Azure Communication Services SMS Java SDK documentation skill. The static analyzer flagged 40 potential issues, but after evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES: backticks are markdown code block syntax (not shell commands), URLs are placeholder examples in documentation, and code references are standard error handling patterns. No security risks identified.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Flag (FALSE POSITIVE)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at lines 3, 246, 279. After review: these are YAML frontmatter, markdown table syntax, and documentation text. No cryptographic code exists in this skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines contain YAML delimiters, markdown table pipes, and plain text - no cryptographic algorithms present

medium_findings.title: External Commands Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 27 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are markdown code block delimiters (```java) in documentation, not actual shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks are markdown syntax for code blocks, not shell command execution. No shell commands are present in this documentation file.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 4 'Hardcoded URL' instances. These are placeholder example URLs in documentation (e.g., https://<resource>.communication.azure.com), not actual network connections.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs contain <resource> placeholders indicating they are example endpoints, not actual connection targets

medium_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'system reconnaissance' at lines 209, 214, 225, 228-230. These are legitimate error handling code examples (handling HTTP status codes, webhook handlers for delivery reports).
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code references are standard SMS delivery report handling and error status checking, common in SMS API integrations

content.user_title: Send SMS with Azure Communication Services
content.value_statement: Send SMS messages from Java applications using Azure Communication Services. Supports single messages, bulk sending, delivery reports, and OTP notifications.
actual_capabilities: Send SMS to single recipient with delivery confirmation
Send SMS to multiple recipients in batch
Configure delivery reports via Azure Event Grid
Support async operations for high-throughput scenarios
Handle error responses and HTTP status codes
Use AzureKeyCredential or connection string authentication
limitations: Phone numbers must be in E.164 format
Rate limits apply based on Azure subscription tier
Delivery reports require Event Grid configuration

use_cases.title: Two-Factor Authentication
use_cases.description: Send OTP codes for user verification and account security

use_cases.title: Transaction Notifications
use_cases.description: Send order confirmations, shipping updates, and payment alerts

use_cases.title: Marketing Campaigns
use_cases.description: Send bulk promotional messages to customer segments

prompt_templates.title: Basic SMS Send
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-communication-sms-java skill to send a verification code '+14255551234' from my Azure ACS number
prompt_templates.scenario: Send a simple SMS message to one recipient

prompt_templates.title: Bulk SMS with Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-communication-sms-java skill to send a flash sale notification to multiple recipients with delivery reporting enabled
prompt_templates.scenario: Send SMS to multiple recipients with custom options

prompt_templates.title: Async Bulk Send
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-communication-sms-java skill to send async bulk messages to a list of phone numbers with tagging for tracking

prompt_templates.title: Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-communication-sms-java skill to show how to handle rate limiting errors and invalid phone numbers when sending SMS
prompt_templates.scenario: Implement proper error handling for SMS operations

output_examples.output: Message sent successfully. Message ID: abc123, Status: Successful
Delivery Report: Message delivered to +14255551234 at 2024-01-15T10:30:00Z

best_practices: Always use E.164 phone number format (+[country code][number])
Enable delivery reports for critical messages like OTPs
Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for 429 errors
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode connection strings in source code - use environment variables
Do not send SMS synchronously in high-traffic APIs without queuing
Do not skip error handling - individual recipients can fail while others succeed

faq.question: What authentication methods are supported?
faq.answer: Three methods: DefaultAzureCredential (recommended), AzureKeyCredential, and connection string.

faq.question: What phone number format is required?
faq.answer: E.164 format is required, for example +14255550100.

faq.question: How do I enable delivery reports?
faq.answer: Set deliveryReportEnabled=true in SmsSendOptions and configure an Event Grid subscription.

faq.question: What happens if a message fails for one recipient?
faq.answer: Other recipients still receive their messages. Check isSuccessful() for each result.

faq.question: Is there rate limiting?
faq.answer: Yes, Azure applies rate limits based on your subscription. Handle 429 responses with retry.

faq.question: Can I send to international numbers?
faq.answer: Yes, but you must enable toll-free messaging in your Azure subscription and use E.164 format.
